tata.FY2023-24: AMBIGUOUS: FY2023 is consolidated while FY2024 is standalone

sections.about: This metric measures total waste recovered through recycling, re-use and other recovery operations under BRSR Principle 6, Essential Indicator on waste recovered. Recovery includes preparing waste to fulfil a purpose in place of new material, including recycling and preparation for re-use. A higher value indicates better performance.
sections.performanceAndDrivers: Tata Steel led in all three years, with FY2025 recovery of 19,438,386 MT versus JSW at 16,546,750 MT, SAIL at 15,365,364 MT and Jindal at 2,018,038 MT. Tata’s recovery exceeds its current-year waste generated because legacy stock can also be recovered or disposed; Tata explicitly notes that recovered/disposed waste may be higher than generated waste due to legacy stock. SAIL shows strong recovery through “other recovery operations,” consistent with its 4R approach and use of slag, mill scale and fines in cement, sinter and steelmaking routes.
sections.tataPositioning: Tata ranked 1st in FY2025; JSW was the closest peer and was about 14.9% behind Tata. Tata held rank 1 across all three years, indicating strong circularity and co-product recovery despite scale and BF-BOF waste volumes.
sections.targets: SAIL targets maximising solid waste utilisation to 100%, and JSL is working toward Zero-Waste-to-Landfill certification by 2030.
sections.comparabilityNotes: Recovery can exceed current-year waste generation because old stock may be recovered in the reporting year; Tata and SAIL both disclose legacy/old-stock effects. Tata Steel FY2022-23 data is reported on a consolidated basis rather than standalone.
